sanskrit_devanagari:
मामुपेत्य पुनर्जन्म दुःखालयमशाश्वतम् ।
नाप्नुवन्ति महात्मानः संसिद्धिं परमां गताः ॥ १५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
mām upetya punar janma duḥkhālayam aśāśvatam |
nāpnuvanti mahātmānaḥ saṃsiddhiṃ paramāṃ gatāḥ || 15 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical; vedabase renders verse as concatenated text but body matches the canonical reading word-for-word).
exact (Mukundananda house style: h-aspirate marks on śh, single danda visarga; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_08.015 = MBh_06,030.015). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: After attaining Me, the great souls, who are yogīs in devotion, never return to this temporary world, which is full of miseries, because they have attained the highest perfection.
BG 8.15: Having attained Me, the great souls are no more subject to rebirth in this world, which is transient and full of misery, because they have attained the highest perfection.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Since this temporary material world is full of the miseries of birth, old age, disease and death, naturally he who achieves the highest perfection and attains the supreme planet, Kṛṣṇaloka, Goloka Vṛndāvana, does not wish to return.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use, ≤300 chars of full bhāṣya per IP rules)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.15 Upetya mam, as a result of reaching Me who am God — as a result of realizing My nature; mahatmanah, the exalted ones, the monks; gatah, who have attained; the paramam, highest; samsiddhim, perfection, called Liberation; na, do not; apnuvanti, get; this kind of punarjanama, rebirth.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 8.15 Having attained Me, they are not subject to rebirth, which leads to a condition that is transient and an abode of sorrow. These great souls, i.e., men of noble minds, worship and attain Me as the supreme perfection, called Liberation.

positions.rendering: the highest perfection, called Liberation; identification with Brahman, the cessation of return-to-birth
supreme perfection through worshipful continuous relation with Krishna; not subject to the rebirth that leads to a transient abode of sorrow
the highest perfection of life — reaching the spiritual abode (Goloka or Vaikuṇṭha), beyond the material rebirth-cycle

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The three major traditions agree on the structural claim (supreme perfection ⇒ no rebirth) and disagree on the destination's content. The disagreement is not adjudicated by the verse and is preserved at source-pack level.

literal_meaning: Having reached Me, the great-souled ones — having attained the supreme perfection — do not obtain rebirth, this impermanent abode of misery.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse describes the consequence of reaching Krishna by the practice the previous verses name. The mahātmānaḥ (great-souled, the ascetic-monks per Shankara; men of noble minds per Ramanuja) attain saṃsiddhi paramā — supreme perfection, identified by all commentators with mokṣa / liberation. They do not return to punar-janma — rebirth — characterized as duḥkhālayam (abode of misery) and aśāśvatam (transient). The traditions all read the verse as a soteriological claim: the supreme perfection eliminates rebirth. They differ on what the perfection consists of (Shankara: identification with Brahman; Ramanuja: continuous worshipful relation; Prabhupada/Gaudiya: residence in the supreme abode). The verse's metaphysical scope vastly exceeds any operational analog the engineering layer can carry.
